# Description

This is a sepia-toned photograph showing a horse-drawn delivery wagon on a city street. A light-colored horse is harnessed to pull a wooden cart with large spoked wheels. The cart carries two people and is loaded with what appears to be product boxes and containers. A storefront is visible in the background with signage that reads "BOTTLERS SUPPLIES" and "Real FLAVOR & EXTRACT," along with partial text mentioning "MILK" and "ORANGE ADE." A man in a white shirt and cap stands on an elevated platform at the rear of the cart, while another person sits lower on the vehicle. The storefront behind the cart shows display windows and architectural details typical of early 20th-century commercial buildings. The photograph appears to be a historical document of a milk or beverage delivery operation, mounted on a cream-colored backing with marked edges consistent with archival presentation.
Collection name: Jesse Brown Cook Scrapbooks Documenting San Francisco History and Law Enforcement

# Stereoscopic Card Description

This is a vintage stereoscopic card (stereograph) numbered 58 and 21084, printed by Keystone View Company. The card displays two nearly identical sepia-toned images side by side, a format designed to create a three-dimensional effect when viewed through a stereoscope. The images show a woman in early 20th-century dress feeding ice cream to a small black bear cub. The woman wears a dark coat with white gloves and appears to be extending a spoon or utensil toward the bear's mouth. The bear stands on its hind legs. Behind them is a brick structure with a mesh or latticed panel. Vegetation and foliage are visible in the background. The caption at the bottom reads "Mrs. Herbert Hoover Feeds Ice Cream to a Small Black Bear Cub, Seward, Alaska." Text on the left margin reads "Keystone View Company, Meadville, Pennsylvania" with additional publication information on the right margin. A grayscale tone reference strip appears at the top of the card.

# Description

This black and white photograph shows a street scene in what appears to be Paris, featuring a group of approximately twenty people gathered in the center of a cobblestone courtyard or plaza. The crowd is assembled around what looks like a street vendor or small market stall with an umbrella or canopy. On the left side of the image stands a man in a coat and hat observing the gathering from a distance.

The buildings surrounding the plaza are Parisian architecture featuring rusticated stone facades on the ground level with large arched and rectangular windows fitted with shutters. A prominent storefront sign reading "COIFFEUR" (hairdresser) is visible on the middle building's facade. Above the storefronts are ornate wrought iron balconies and additional levels with regularly spaced windows. The buildings to the right show varied construction styles, including brick elements and stone decoration.

The scene captures a moment of everyday urban life, with well-dressed figures in period clothing, suggesting this photograph was taken sometime in the mid-20th century. The architectural details and street layout are characteristic of a Parisian neighborhood or courtyard area.

# Description

This vintage postcard depicts Sdreckels Rotisserie, a San Francisco dining establishment located on the 15th floor of the Call Building, owned by A. Wolff. The card features an ornate gold-framed header with the business name against a dark background. The main illustration shows an elegant interior dining scene with two figures seated at a table set with wine bottles, glasses, and a floral centerpiece. The woman on the right wears a pink dress, while the man on the left wears dark clothing. Large windows frame an expansive cityscape view of San Francisco's densely packed buildings stretching into the distance. In the lower left, an inset image displays the Call Building itself, a tall ornate structure with a distinctive domed cupola, labeled "CAFE." The bottom of the card includes text reading "ICE CREAM PARLOR - CAFE AND RESTAURANT 250 FEET ABOVE THE BAY." The publisher credit in the upper right corner identifies Edward H. Mitchell as the publisher from San Francisco. Decorative plant elements in terracotta pots appear in the lower corners of the dining scene.
Collection name: Walter Robert and Gail Lynn Kransky collection of Edward H. Mitchell postcards

# Description

This hand-tinted historical postcard depicts the Los Angeles Pigeon Farm near Elysian Park. The image shows a large open ground area densely covered with white pigeons in the foreground. Behind the birds are several wooden structures including farm buildings and what appears to be feeding or housing facilities with railings. A long wooden corridor or walkway with railings extends across the middle ground. To the left, a water channel or irrigation ditch runs along the property. The background features evergreen trees, utility poles, hills, and distant buildings. The sky displays muted colors typical of hand-coloring processes from the early 20th century. The postcard has aged edges and shows wear consistent with vintage materials. At the bottom is a metric color reference scale used for archival documentation. The text at the top reads "Los Angeles Pigeon Farm, Near Elysian Park" in red letters.
